data.title: Europe must separate legitimate security concerns from politics
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
As Dutch officials prepare to meet their Chinese counterparts in Beijing to discuss the Nexperia dispute, Europe faces a larger question. How did a routine investment review turn into a diplomatic incident between trading partners who once championed the “rules-based order”?
Western leaders say they are not at war, yet every rule of wartime economics now applies. Company management is seized, trade is blocked and neutral countries are threatened with secondary sanctions if they refuse to take sides. The language remains diplomatic, but the instruments are those of war.
Europe now risks undermining the very legal predictability that once made it attractive to global investors.
Beijing has protested, and it bears restating that every action the Chinese government has taken has been both mild and consistent with Western diplomatic practice. In response to an ongoing series of Dutch export bans and with the addition of this latest takeover, it would not have been unheard of for diplomatic relations to be downgraded.
The Chinese export ban was proportional and moderate, and the Nexperia case shows how easily “de-risking” can become “self-risking” when EU member states act first and coordinate later. Germany’s auto sector and Europe’s semiconductor ambitions both depend on continued market access to China; punitive gestures do nothing to improve security or resilience.
If Europe truly seeks strategic autonomy, it must separate legitimate security concerns from headline-driven politics. A continent built on commercial law cannot preserve its prosperity by rewriting those laws on the fly. Attempts to bully China or other nations because they do not toe the EU line will only result in further incidents. Sovereign nations are not European colonies in this century.
Erik Cota-Robles, Helsinki, Finland
Malaysia is right to challenge court’s damages ruling
I refer to the article, “Malaysia to appeal against damages ruling for families of abducted activists” (November 6).
High Court judge Su Tiang Joo has ordered the Malaysian government and police to pay more than 37 million ringgit (US$9 million) to the family of Pastor Raymond Koh for his enforced disappearance and more than 3 million ringgit to the family of activist Amri Che Mat for negligence in a police investigation.
According to the judge, the compensation to Koh’s family was “for deprivation of personal liberty under the Federal Constitution”, ruling that the federal government was liable for breaching its statutory duty and acting unconstitutionally.
That is constitutional tort. American law professor Marshall Shapo first discussed constitutional tort law – suits brought against government officials for constitutional violations – in a 1965 paper. However, it was not until 1978 that the term was used in the US Supreme Court in Monell v. Department of Social Services, a landmark case that established that local governments can be held liable for constitutional violations that result from their official policies or customs, rather than just the actions of individual employees.
In Malaysia, as noted in the Malaysia report of Civil Liability for Human Rights Violations: A Handbook for Practitioners, while it is arguably open for victims to seek civil remedies for violation of a fundamental right under the Federal Constitution, the application of constitutional tort “has not yet been determined decisively”.
Thus, Malaysian courts have yet to fully endorse infringements of the Federal Constitution as giving rise to a distinct cause of action, with that possibility having only been considered in minority Federal Court decisions in connection with claims for deaths in custody.
The Attorney General’s Chambers said it would challenge the court decisions on Koh and Amri. It should, given that Malaysian courts have yet to fully endorse constitutional tort.
Mohamad Hafiz Hassan, lecturer, Faculty of Law, Multimedia University, Malaysia
Patient records solution close at hand
Your editorial of November 9, “More private-sector buy-in is needed to enhance eHealth”, addresses the sharing of private doctors’ patient records with the Hospital Authority (HA) through the eHealth platform. You noted that some patients were reluctant to give consent to private practitioners for their records to be uploaded.
One way of addressing such issues seems ready to hand. It is common practice for patients to have various tests and scans performed privately in advance of a relatively distant scheduled appointment at an HA hospital.
These patients present themselves at the HA hospital with their test results in hand. Given patient consent during their HA appointment, these results could, on such occasions, be uploaded to the HA system. Perhaps the HA could consider a protocol for implementing such an idea.
Gillian Bickley, Discovery Bay, Lantau

data.title: DNA research suggests Hitler likely had condition limiting sexual development
data.description:
Adolf Hitler most likely suffered from the genetic condition Kallmann Syndrome that can manifest itself in undescended testicles and a micropenis, researchers and documentary makers said on Thursday, following DNA testing of the Nazi dictator’s blood.
The new research also quashes the suggestion that Hitler had Jewish ancestry.
Popular World War II songs often mocked Hitler’s anatomy but lacked any scientific basis.
The findings by an international team of scientists and historians now appear to confirm long-standing suspicions around his sexual development.
“No one has ever really been able to explain why Hitler was so uncomfortable around women throughout his life, or why he probably never entered into intimate relations with women,” said Alex Kay of the University of Potsdam.
“But now we know that he had Kallmann Syndrome, this could be the answer we’ve been looking for,” he said.

The research findings are featured in a new documentary, Hitler’s DNA: Blueprint of a Dictator, due to be broadcast on Saturday.
The testing found a “high likelihood” that Hitler had Kallmann Syndrome and “very high” scores - in the top 1 per cent - for a predisposition to autism, schizophrenia and bipolar disorder, programme makers Blink Films said.
The research team stressed that such conditions, however, could not explain or excuse Hitler’s warmongering or racist policies.
Over 50 million people are estimated to have died in World War II, including 6 million Jews who were systematically murdered.
The testing was made possible after researchers obtained a sample of Hitler’s blood from a piece of material taken from the sofa on which he shot himself.
Kallmann Syndrome often results in “low testosterone levels, undescended testicles and can result in a micropenis”, Blink Films said.
The DNA results additionally rule out the possibility that Hitler had a Jewish grandfather via his grandmother, who was rumoured to have got pregnant by an employer in whose house she worked.
“Analysis of the DNA debunks this myth by showing that the Y chromosome data matches the DNA of Hitler’s male line relative. If he had Jewish ancestry (through an outside relationship), that match wouldn’t be there,” the production company added.
Geneticist Turi King, known for identifying the remains of medieval king Richard III and who also worked on the project, said Hitler’s genes put him in a category of people who were often sent to the gas chambers by the Nazis.
“Hitler’s policies are completely around eugenics,” said the expert in ancient and forensic DNA at the University of Bath in western England.
“If he had been able to look at his own DNA... he almost certainly would have sent himself,” she said.
The two-part documentary is scheduled to begin on the UK’s Channel 4 on Saturday.

data.title: Hong Kong stocks slide most in a week on soft China economic data, fading US rate cut bets
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks fell by the most in a week, triggered by China’s sluggish economic data, uncertainty over an interest-rate cut in the US and lofty valuations of artificial intelligence companies.
The Hang Seng Index slipped 0.9 per cent to 26,833.92 as of 10.21am local time. The Hang Seng Tech Index dropped 1.7 per cent. On the mainland, the CSI 300 Index slid 0.7 per cent and the Shanghai Composite Index retreated 0.2 per cent.
E-commerce giant JD.com slumped 4.1 per cent to HK$119.30 after third-quarter profit fell 55 per cent from a year ago. Peer Alibaba Group Holding dropped 2.8 per cent to HK$157.30. Tencent Holdings shed 2.1 per cent to HK$642.50 even after its quarterly results beat estimates and Meituan lost 1.8 per cent to HK$100.30.
China’s industrial production climbed 4.9 per cent last month from a year earlier, the National Bureau of Statistics said on Friday. The median forecast in a Bloomberg survey was for an increase of 5.5 per cent.
Retail sales gained 2.9 per cent, slowing for the fifth straight month in the longest such decline since 2021. Fixed-asset investment shrank 1.7 per cent in the first 10 months of the year, a record for the period.
Also, the decline in China’s home prices deepened last month.
“Economic activity slowed modestly in October” said Zhang Zhiwei, chief economist at Pinpoint Asset Management in Hong Kong. “The drop of fixed asset investment was quite fast, reflecting the weak property sector investment as well as lacklustre infrastructure investment.”
“Nonetheless, China is on track to achieve the growth target of 5 per cent, so I don’t think we can expect stimulus for the rest of this year. I think fiscal policy support will come in the first quarter.”
US stocks tumbled overnight, with the Nasdaq 100 sliding more than 2 per cent, as a hawkish rhetoric on monetary policy by US Federal Reserve officials pushed the odds of a rate cut next month below 50 per cent. A dimmer outlook for financial easing poses a threat to the crowded trade in technology stocks.
Elsewhere in Asia-Pacific, Japan’s Nikkei 225 slipped 1.5 per cent, South Korea’s Kospi tumbled 2.4 per cent and Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 lost 1.4 per cent.

data.title: Serial intruder grabs Ariana Grande at Wicked premiere in Singapore
data.description:
Cynthia Erivo performed some real magic on Thursday when she saved Wicked co-star Ariana Grande from an overeager “troll” at the premiere of the blockbuster’s second chapter in Singapore.
The Oscar nominees were walking down a yellow brick road-inspired carpet, holding hands with co-star Michelle Yeoh and posing for fans, when a man screamed and hopped the barricade, according to fan footage. The man then wrapped an arm around 32-year-old Grande, only for Erivo, 38, and a guard to cut in.
Erivo then encased the pop star in a protective hug at the Universal Studios Singapore event, as did Academy Award winner Yeoh, 63.
TMZ reported that the man – who was ultimately escorted out by security, encouraged by other fans’ booing – is likely the self-proclaimed “troll” known as Pyjama Man, who posted a 10-second clip of the interaction on TikTok.

Grande is not the first to find herself in the viral clutches of Pyjama Man’s stunts, which tend to involve barging on to a stage, much to the shock of unsuspecting performers, which have included the Weeknd and the Chainsmokers.
Pyjama Man has over 9,300 followers on Instagram, where his profile is verified and his bio proclaims, “Ariana Grande is the Queen”.
Wicked: For Good hits cinemas on November 21, concluding Jon M. Chu’s two-part adaptation of the beloved Broadway musical and Gregory McGuire’s bestselling book from which it takes its name.

data.title: 5 of the best things to do in Hong Kong this weekend, November 14-16
data.description:
The cooler temperatures make for the perfect time to explore Hong Kong’s vibrant outdoor events across the city.
Whether it is a rooftop beer festival, a tournament for a cause, a doggy wonderland or a live street art showcase, there is every way to enjoy the weather this weekend.
Read on to find out more.
1. Tong Chong Street Market Beer Festival












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by mhp by Tong Chong Street Market (@tcsmarkethk)



This Saturday is the last day of the Tong Chong Street Market Beer Festival, taking place on the 37th floor of One Island East in Quarry Bay on Hong Kong Island. The event at the new dining destination, Mhp by Tong Chong Street Market, will offer three hours of bottomless craft beers from eight local breweries, paired with a special festival menu, live DJ sets and beer-themed games.
This year’s festival highlights the vibrancy of the local craft brewing scene with exclusive launches, including the Green Light Pilsner, a honeydew melon-infused brew created for the event and Young Master Brewery’s 12 Shui Wild Sour Ale.
For more information and registration, click here.
Mhp by Tong Chong Street Market, 37/ F, One Island East, Taikoo Place, 18 Westlands Road, Quarry Bay
2. Doggo Fest

Doggo Fest will be taking over the Great Lawn at the West Kowloon Cultural District’s Art Park over the weekend, turning the space into a playground for pups and their owners. The event features a giant ball pit, foam party and photo ops for professional pet portraits. At the same time, human companions can enjoy live music, a dedicated restaurant and nearly 100 vendor booths at the Doggo Expo.
Following an overwhelming early-bird response that saw several sessions sell out, general ticket sales operate on a one-person, one-dog policy, though the expo area remains free. All dogs must present a valid dog license or vaccination record for entry.
For more information and ticketing, click here.
Great Lawn, Art Park, West Kowloon Cultural District
3. Paws United Charity Pickleball Competition












View this post on Instagram

























Paws United Charity (PUC) will host its inaugural PUC Pickleball Charity Competition on November 15 and 16 at the Yama Pickleball Arena. The event aims to rally community support and raise funds for the new PUC Academy, scheduled to open in June 2026. This facility will expand the charity’s capacity for animal rescue, rehabilitation and public education programmes.
The competition will feature performances and participation from a roster of celebrity supporters, including Alfred Hui, Chiu Fung of C AllStar and other top Hong Kong players.
Yama Pickleball Arena, 9/F, Landmark South, 39 Yip Kan Street, Wong Chuk Hang
4. MOS Festival












View this post on Instagram

























The Meeting of Styles (MOS) festival, launched in Germany nearly three decades ago, will be held in Hong Kong for the first time, featuring more than 80 artists from across the globe. Together, they will transform the venue into a dynamic open-air gallery and a landmark moment for the local creative scene.
This weekend will mark the last two days of the five-day programme, where visitors can watch murals created in real time alongside competitive graffiti battles, educational workshops and panel discussions. The event seeks to shift the global dialogue of contemporary urban art, elevating the discourse around street art and offering the public a front-row seat to its creative process.
Rooftop, D2 Place ONE, 9 Cheung Yee Street, Lai Chi Kok
5. Kokuho


Anchored by an astonishing central performance from Ryo Yoshizawa and a fastidious devotion to the traditional art of kabuki theatre, Lee Sang-il’s sumptuous saga Kokuho emerges as the finest Japanese film of the year.
Spanning five decades in the life of a celebrated onnagata – a male performer who specialises in female roles – the film chronicles the endless dedication and heart-wrenching sacrifices required to reach the pinnacle of this bewitching art form, and the toll it takes on the individual.
Earlier this year, Kokuho became the first live-action Japanese film in more than two decades to surpass 10 billion yen (US$65 million) at the box office. Read our full review
Kokuho is in cinemas now.
Find this useful? Look out for our recommendations every Friday online.
Like what you read? Follow SCMP Lifestyle on Facebook, X and Instagram. You can also sign up for our eNewsletter here.

data.title: How Singapore’s bid for green shipping leadership is gaining traction, with China’s help
data.description:
Singapore’s ambitions to lead the world in green shipping are picking up steam, with a new agreement with China poised to enhance the city state’s decarbonisation efforts and cement its role as a key hub for alternative fuels.
The partnership, signed last month, aims to establish a digital and green shipping corridor between the two countries, and promote maritime decarbonisation, innovation and greater supply chain efficiency.
The deal could give Singapore a competitive edge by plugging it directly into China’s rapidly expanding clean‑fuel production ecosystem, at a time when Beijing is emerging as the key driver of global maritime decarbonisation, according to analysts.
“The arrangement will allow Singapore to build a strong supply chain with alternative fuels and be a major supplier of biofuel, green methanol, green ammonia and maybe even liquid natural gas,” said Jason Xu, director of the Maritime Energy and Sustainable Development Centre of Excellence (MESD) at Nanyang Technological University.
The large volume of shipping traffic between both countries meant that even small shifts to green fuels or improvements in operational efficiency would lead to substantial emissions cuts, said Liu Ming, research director at MESD.
Given that Singapore is the world’s biggest bunkering hub and home to the second-busiest container port, analysts say it is no surprise that the city state is leading Southeast Asia and even the world in driving green shipping efforts.
Singapore supplies over a sixth of the fuels used by global shipping, according to its Maritime and Port Authority, delivering a record 55 million tonnes last year. The country also supplied a record of over 1 million tonnes of alternative bunker fuels in 2024.
The city state has also signed memorandums to establish digital and green shipping corridors with countries such as South Korea, India and Japan, as well as major ports in Los Angeles and Rotterdam.
“Singapore is in a very strategic and unique position to push [for] maritime decarbonisation,” said Liu Nengye, a law professor at the Singapore Management University (SMU) specialising in ocean and sustainable law.
“It can help not only in decarbonising ship emissions, but also in developing infrastructure for the transport and storage of sustainable fuels, which are not widely available yet. Singapore is capable of becoming a global leader in this space.”

Long road to decarbonisation
Shipping accounts for about 3 per cent of global greenhouse gas emissions currently, but this could rise to 5-8 per cent if no action is taken, according to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change.
In 2023, the International Maritime Organisation (IMO) set targets including net-zero emissions from international shipping by 2050. IMO is aiming for a 40 per cent reduction in carbon dioxide emissions by 2030 from 2008 levels, and for green fuels and technologies to comprise at least 5 per cent of energy sources.
To help those targets, Singapore last year supplied 1.34 million tonnes of alternative fuels, 880,000 tonnes of biofuel, 460,000 tonnes of liquefied natural gas and 1,626 tonnes of methanol. Ammonia, which recorded a supply of 9.74 tonnes, was bunkered for the first time globally in the city state.
But progress in decarbonisation has been hampered by national economic interests and green solutions in their infancy.
For instance, oil producers such as Indonesia would find it a challenge to switch to greener fuels, said Ho Hiang Kwee, an adjunct senior research fellow at the Energy Studies Institute, citing the protests after Jakarta removed a fossil fuel subsidy in 2022.
Experts note that shipping firms are facing a big challenge in plotting their decarbonisation pathway, given the early-stage development of green fuels and technologies.
Indra Overland, head of the centre for energy research at the Norwegian Institute of International Affairs, cited ammonia’s toxicity and flammability as an example, saying countries risked “betting on the wrong technologies”.
Lynn Loo, CEO of the Global Centre for Maritime Decarbonisation, said new and alternative fuels, such as ammonia and methanol, were significantly more expensive than fossil fuels.
Loo said an ammonia-powered ship would take about two years to build, and businesses had to factor this timeline into their risk assessment.
Other emerging technologies, such as carbon capture and storage, whereby emissions from fossil fuels are captured before being released into the atmosphere, are also expensive, and their technical feasibility is still being assessed, according to Loo.
“Unless the shipowners are convinced there is a pathway to get rid of the carbon dioxide, never mind it costs more, nobody is going to adopt it.”
Shipping firms, such as Singapore-based Eastern Pacific Shipping and French giant CMA CGM, have signed agreements with the city state for collaborative efforts on exploring and advancing the use of green fuels and technologies. EPS also pledged to register six vessels with ammonia dual-fuel engines under the Singapore flag from 2026.
The global industry’s decarbonisation efforts also suffered a setback over a delay in the launch of IMO’s net-zero framework, under which a carbon tax would be imposed on shipping companies. US President Donald Trump has threatened to impose tariffs on countries that voted in favour of the carbon tax.
Originally targeted for launch in 2028, 176 IMO member states, including Singapore, voted last month to delay discussions on the framework’s implementation by a year. When implemented, the tax is expected to raise US$10 billion a year to help developing countries transition to cleaner fuels.

China stepping in
With the US seemingly falling back into the era of fossil fuels, the spotlight has turned to China to help drive decarbonisation initiatives.
Overland told This Week in Asia he was confident China would make tremendous contributions to green shipping.
“It has already established a massive manufacturing capacity for clean technology and dramatically reduced costs, and its global contribution will probably grow even further,” he said.
Liu from SMU noted that Southeast Asian countries could potentially lean into China for technology and finance to drive sustainable shipping.
Malaysia on Monday reaffirmed its partnership with China on advancing its transition to a low-carbon and sustainable maritime industry, and Indonesia last year agreed to advance a tie-up where the superpower committed to support its transition to clean energy.
The Association of Southeast Asian Nations last month upgraded its free-trade agreement with China, which is expected to expand cooperation in green and digital economies and sustainable supply chain connectivity.
But experts say it is not sufficient to rely on one superpower to drive sustainability.
As an import-dependent country, Singapore should maintain strategic relationships with China and other countries it buys low carbon fuels from, according to Liu from MESD. He also urged policymakers to push for the use of alternative fuels.
Experts also called on policymakers and regulators to impose enforcement measures and incentives to spur shipowners to decarbonise.
For ships which adopt green solutions, Singapore waives registration fees and tonnage taxes for those registered under its flag, and port fees for vessels which call at its port.
While the industry’s road to net-zero was “promising”, achieving it would require efforts from both private companies and policymakers worldwide, said SMU’s Liu.
“It’s not just on ships to follow rules, there needs to be checked when they call at a port by the authorities to make sure the ships followed the rules at sea.”

data.title: China’s economy posts mixed results in October as fixed-asset investment declines further
data.description:
China’s economic indicators posted mixed results in October as markets were gripped by uncertainty ahead of the late-month summit between the US and Chinese presidents.
National fixed-asset investment fell by 1.7 per cent, widening from the 0.5 per cent drop recorded in the first nine months and underperforming Wind’s forecast of a 0.71 per cent decrease.
Retail sales, a major gauge of consumer spending, grew by 2.9 per cent year on year in October, according to data released by the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) on Friday. The figure surpassed the 2.73 per cent forecast from financial data provider Wind and marked a slight drop from September’s 3 per cent increase.
Industrial output rose by 4.9 per cent in October, slowing from September’s 6.5 per cent expansion and lower than the 5.52 per cent estimate by Wind.
More to follow...

data.title: Exploring Africa’s extremes on a luxury safari through Namibia and Botswana
data.description:
I have never been to Mars, but I imagine this is what it would look like. It’s been a long, at times arduous journey but after a somewhat terrifying one-hour flight in a cramped four-seater propeller plane, I have arrived at a private airstrip surrounded by nothing except sand and craggy, granite mountains. We are on the eastern edge of the Namib Desert. It very much lives up to its name that in the local Nama language means “an area where there is nothing”. One of the world’s oldest, dating back at least 55 million years, the Namib is fabled for its towering sand dunes that stretch west all the way to the wild Atlantic coast.
At first glance, it’s hard to believe that anything can survive here, but it’s home to an astonishing group of desert-adapted wildlife that has thrived for millions of years. I’m here to embark on a different type of safari – one that is a far cry from the Big 5 (the supposed “must-sees” of lion, leopard, elephant, rhino and buffalo) or exhilarating game drives that have long made Africa a bucket-list destination. A short drive away is my home for the next few days, andBeyond Sossusvlei Desert Lodge, which sits on a private reserve spanning over 12,700 hectares. It’s designed to blend into the dunescape that envelops it, and the glass and rock main building is topped with rust-coloured “canopies” that make a statement in sustainable design. This philosophy continues through to the 10 stand-alone suites, all of which have solar panels and water treatment systems.

Encased entirely in glass, each 893 sq ft villa has an expansive living room, fireplace, indoor and outdoor showers and a generous private pool. No matter where you stand – bathroom included – you can bask in unobstructed desert vistas.
Luxury amenities aside, it’s the small, thoughtful details that stand out. A special hatch allows the butler to make discreet deliveries without my ever having to open the door. I can mix my own cocktails at the bespoke in-room bar stocked with every spirit imaginable plus freshly made snacks. There are also watercolours, paintbrushes and crayons for when I am feeling artistic, and a retractable roof ideal for stargazing.
As with traditional safaris, most activities take place in the early morning and late afternoon. There’s a huge variety on offer, ranging from outdoor bush breakfasts complete with table service and shakshuka cooked on a charcoal grill, to romantic sundowners on the dunes. Then you have the adventures – hot air ballooning, helicopter rides, ATV excursions, guided hikes and visits to ancient caves decorated with paintings by the San people, considered the oldest collection of indigenous groups in Africa.

High on everyone’s list is a visit to Namib-Naukluft National Park, which features some of the world’s largest and oldest sand dunes, including the tallest in the area, Big Daddy, at over 325 metres (1,066ft) high. Those with iron will and buns of steel can choose to climb its steep ridges. The sand seems to slide away underneath your every step, making the trek feel almost futile.
It’s still a worthwhile experience though, to take in panoramic views of colossal, undulating dunes spread out beneath you. I don’t think it can get any better until I slide down the dune barefoot, feeling the cool sand beneath my toes. At the bottom, I explore Deadvlei, a large pan of cracked clay, where 900-year-old petrified trees stand like a surreal scene from a Tim Burton movie.
While my visit to the dunes is epic, so is watching nature unfold. After a few days I can identify the piercing calls of the male korhaan bird. I squeal like a little girl when I spot a rare mountain zebra galloping across the desert plain like a mythical unicorn. Watching wild ostrich, oryx and springbok at the watering hole outside my suite feels more entertaining than any Netflix series. Night is another spectacle as the skies come alive. With the lodge located within one of the world’s few dark-sky reserves – an area that restricts light pollution – I can spot the Milky Way and shooting stars with my naked eye before heading to the on-site observatory, where a resident astrologer points out constellations and far-flung galaxies.

My head is in the clouds again a few days later as I board another (thankfully, bigger) plane to my next, utterly different destination: the water world of Botswana’s Okavango Delta.
A Unesco World Heritage site, this famed inland delta in the heart of the Kalahari Desert is fed by annual floods that come from as far away as Angola, attracting a staggering array of wildlife – along with the likes of Prince Harry and Meghan Markle, and other A-listers. Here, nature dictates the landscape, from the flowing waters to the herds of elephants that create pathways allowing other fauna to thrive. The action begins even before I get off the plane. Our pilot informs us that we cannot land as a herd of zebras is blocking the airstrip. Then the resort’s jeep hits traffic in the form of antelopes, elephants, giraffes and baboons. The local watering hole is more crowded than Times Square on a weekend.
To enjoy both the lush waterways and drier terrain, I am staying at two different lodges that promise bush walks and water safaris. My first stop, andBeyond Nxabega, is a contemporary take on traditional tented camps. Each of the nine suites is set on raised wooden platforms under a canopy of ebony trees and features modern comforts such as en suite toilets, a personal bar, and indoor and outdoor showers. The swinging daybed on the private veranda is my favourite spot and overlooks a watering hole teeming with impalas, warthogs and other creatures.

While Nxabega has the Big 5 – including an elusive male rhino in heat – its prime location gives guests the unique opportunity to explore the delta via its waterways, an experience that can often be meditative and occasionally exhilarating. We glide through the still, shallow waters in a mokoro (a traditional dugout canoe) to watch the sunset, with bullfrogs chiming in the background. The next morning, I channel Dora the Explorer as we navigate the lagoons by motorboat, zigzagging between crocodiles and hippos bathing, their ears poking out from the water like drones.
It gets even wilder at my final destination, andBeyond’s Sandibe Okavango Safari Lodge. The camp is known for its distinctive architecture, an open-plan design with a roof inspired by the carapace of the pangolin (this anteater, sadly almost poached to extinction for supposed medicinal benefits, was last spotted in the area over a year ago). There’s a spa and gym, plus multilevel dining areas and an interactive kitchen where you can customise everything from omelettes to burgers and pizzas. The 12 suites are also next level – each with a walk-in wardrobe, skylit shower and outdoor deck with plunge pool.
The game drive schedule is not for the faint-hearted, with early starts and long hours of bouncing about on dusty tracks. In this secluded place, wildlife sightings are rarer than on the East African plains, say, but feel more intimate thanks to incredible guides who know the animals and their habits better than their own children.

For the next few days, I feel like I’m running on adrenaline. From tracking two packs of African wild dogs while they stalk then gorge on impalas, to a pride of 20 lions massacring a wild buffalo, and a young leopard jumping to catch a quail mid-air for breakfast, the delta offers more thrills than a theme park. It’s a true wild ride that I feel privileged to witness.
My whirlwind trip showed me how safaris in Africa can transport you to a place that feels beyond time. From the incredible people to the raw dry beauty of the ancient coastal desert and the lush wetlands of the delta, it was a journey of heart, body and spirit that everyone should experience at least once.
The author was a guest of andBeyond, a conservation-led luxury travel company.

data.title: Morgan Stanley faces US scrutiny over Zijin Gold’s Hong Kong IPO
data.description:
Morgan Stanley’s underwriting of Zijin Gold International’s Hong Kong initial public offering (IPO) placed it and its US investors at risk of regulatory, financial and reputational harm, a US House of Representatives committee told the bank on Thursday.
Zijin Gold is a subsidiary of Zijin Mining Group, a global mining company based in China that is on a US government list of companies whose imports are banned over alleged human rights abuses involving Uygurs.
In September, Morgan Stanley assisted with Zijin Gold’s IPO to help its parent company raise funds by selling its non-Chinese gold mining assets and listing them on the Hong Kong stock exchange, the House’s select committee on China said. That raised questions of whether it helped Zijin Mining evade the US prohibitions, according to the committee.
Morgan Stanley declined to comment. Zijin Gold and Zijin Mining did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

“When US financial institutions engage with Chinese firms linked to Uygur forced labour, they undermine the US government’s goal of deterring forced labour globally,” Representative John Moolenaar, the committee’s chair, wrote in a letter to Morgan Stanley CEO Ted Pick.
In January, Zijin Mining was added to the Uygur Forced Labour Prevention Act Entity List, which restricts imports tied to what the US says is an ongoing genocide of minorities in China’s western Xinjiang region.
US officials say Chinese authorities have established labour camps for Uygurs and other Muslim minority groups in Xinjiang. Beijing denies any abuses.
In the letter, Moolenaar seeks documents and communications about Morgan Stanley’s involvement in the public offering related to the company’s links to the Chinese government, Communist Party, military, and human rights abuses. He asked for the information by November 27.
The letter is the latest action by his committee on US financial institutions’ involvement in underwriting IPOs of Chinese companies with ties to the Chinese military or to illegal labour practices.
In July, the committee subpoenaed documents from JPMorgan and Bank of America over their roles in underwriting the Hong Kong IPO of China’s Contemporary Amperex Technology (CATL), the world’s largest electric-vehicle-battery maker. CATL has been designated a Chinese military company by the US Department of Defence.

data.title: Stranded Chinese astronauts to return to Earth aboard new crew’s spacecraft
data.description:
Three astronauts on board China’s Tiangong space station, whose launch back to Earth was delayed last week after suspected space debris struck their return vessel, are set to make their return on Friday on the new crew’s spacecraft.
The Shenzhou-20 crew are expected to land at the Dongfeng Landing Site in the Inner Mongolia autonomous region on Friday aboard the Shenzhou-21 spacecraft, the China Manned Space Agency (CMSA) said.
It said all three astronauts – Chen Dong, Wang Jie and Chen Zhongrui – were in good health and preparations at the landing site were under way for their return.
The new astronauts on the Shenzhou-21, who arrived on November 1, were originally set to make their trip back to Earth next year aboard their own spacecraft. The CMSA said a new spacecraft – the Shenzhou-22 – would soon be sent to the Tiangong.
The three returning astronauts, who have been in orbit since April, had been scheduled to return aboard their own spacecraft on November 5 after completing a handover to the relief crew, who arrived at the space station days before to begin their six-month mission.
On the day the Shenzhou-20 crew was scheduled to make their return, the CMSA said it had been postponed following a suspected impact by small space debris.
The agency said the debris was suspected to have struck the spacecraft and that they were conducting an impact analysis and risk assessment.
According to Chinese space authorities, an emergency rescue system is in place for such incidents, with a backup spacecraft on standby for rapid deployment if necessary.
On Tuesday, the South China Morning Post reported that the Shenzhou-22 – which was originally built for the next crew rotation – was being prepared for launch at the Jiuquan Satellite Launch Centre in the Gobi Desert.
